completeness.rationale: The triage captured most expected items: size-dependent behavior (larger PDFs affected, smaller may not be), timing (~1-2 weeks ago), multiple customers affected, and binary content being mangled (evidenced by file growing larger). The upload proxy / different upload path angle was not explicitly identified, though 'chunked-transfer threshold' was mentioned as a hypothesis. Other file types being potentially affected was noted as an information gap rather than confirmed. The 1MB threshold specifically was not pinpointed.

accuracy.rationale: The base64 encoding hypothesis is not exactly correct (the actual cause is Content-Type stripping leading to text encoding transformation), but the agent correctly identified: (1) an encoding/binary transformation problem, (2) size-dependent behavior pointing to different upload paths, (3) a recent pipeline change as the trigger, and explicitly called out 'any change to Content-Type or Content-Transfer-Encoding headers' in the recommended fix — extremely close to the actual root cause. This satisfies acceptable path 1 (size-dependent behavior, different upload paths) and partially satisfies path 2 (recent update as trigger). The directional accuracy is good.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked three substantive questions and extracted a critical diagnostic clue (downloaded file is larger than original). However, the agent never directly asked about recent deployments or system changes — a key question when a reporter volunteers 'this worked fine a week or two ago.' The exact size threshold was not confirmed. Other file types were asked about but not confirmed. Given the impatient reporter, the agent had limited opportunity but still left some obvious diagnostic paths unexplored.

economy.rationale: Three questions total, all productive. Q1 was slightly broad (drag-and-drop vs. button distinction was low value), but the core content was relevant. Q2 efficiently asked about variability and other file types together. Q3 was an excellent targeted diagnostic test. Minimal wasted turns.

question_quality.rationale: Q3 — asking the reporter to compare file sizes before and after upload — was genuinely insightful. It's not a question most reporters would think to check, and it directly revealed that the file grows in size, a key diagnostic signal pointing to encoding transformation rather than truncation. Q2 was well-targeted in asking about variability across PDFs. Q1 was more generic. Overall the questions demonstrated real diagnostic reasoning.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary is highly actionable: it includes specific areas of the codebase to investigate (Content-Type headers, chunked transfer, storage layer), a proposed test case with multiple file sizes, clear reproduction steps, and a strong root cause hypothesis pointing in the right direction. A developer could immediately start reviewing recent changes to the upload pipeline and checking storage object headers. Minor gap: no explicit callout to check the upload proxy specifically.

notable_strengths: The file size comparison question (Q3) was a clever, non-obvious diagnostic technique that drew out a critical clue — file grows rather than shrinks — pointing directly to a transformation/encoding issue rather than truncation

The recommended fix explicitly listed Content-Type and Content-Transfer-Encoding headers as things to check, landing very close to the actual root cause even though the base64 hypothesis was not precisely correct
The triage summary is well-structured and actionable, with reproduction steps, a proposed test case at multiple file sizes, and clearly enumerated information gaps
Handled an impatient, resistant reporter gracefully while still extracting key diagnostic information
notable_weaknesses: Never directly asked about recent deployments or system changes — the reporter volunteered timing ('a week or two ago') but a targeted question about 'was there a recent update or release?' might have surfaced the proxy update directly
The exact size threshold (1MB) was not confirmed; the agent knew large files were affected but didn't attempt to narrow the boundary
Other file types were not confirmed as affected or unaffected — this remained an open gap that could have been resolved with a simple follow-up test
most_insightful_question: Could you try one quick thing for us? Pick one of the broken PDFs, note its file size on your computer before uploading, then upload it and download it back. Compare the file size of the original to the downloaded copy.
missed_opportunities: Ask directly: 'Was there any recent deployment or update to the upload feature around the time this started?' — this could have surfaced the proxy update
Ask the reporter to try uploading a very small PDF (e.g., under 500KB) and a large one (over 2MB) side by side to confirm the size threshold pattern more precisely
Ask whether other binary file types (images, spreadsheets) over a similar size also exhibit the issue, which would confirm the problem is not PDF-specific and help isolate the upload path
